course.title: Hebrew, Start to Advanced
course.subtitle: Modern Hebrew from absolute beginner through fluent, formal command — thousands of sentences of grammar and conversational practice
course.heroLedeSuffix: Real sentences from lesson one, full niqqud to guide every word, gradually shedding the vowel marks as you climb.

a0-niqqud-vowel-points.title: Niqqud – Vowel Points (נִיקוּד)
a0-niqqud-vowel-points.pattern: consonant letter + niqqud mark = one syllable (e.g. בָּ = "ba", בִּ = "bi", בּוֹ = "bo")
a0-niqqud-vowel-points.explanation: Hebrew letters are mostly consonants — vowels usually aren't written as separate letters, but as small dots and dashes called niqqud, placed above, below, or inside a letter. Each niqqud mark has its own name (kamatz, patach, chirik...) and tells you which vowel sound to add to the consonant right before it. Once you know the niqqud signs, you can sound out almost any Hebrew word, even one you've never seen before.
a0-niqqud-vowel-points.fact: Modern Israelis barely use niqqud in everyday writing — newspapers, novels, street signs, and text messages are printed almost entirely without it, and native readers simply infer the vowels from context and habit. Niqqud survives mainly in prayer books, poetry, dictionaries, and books for children or new readers — which is exactly why this course starts with full niqqud on every word, like training wheels for reading Hebrew.

a0-your-suffix-pronouns.title: "Your" attached to a noun: -ךָ / -ךְ (כִּנּוּיֵי קִנְיָן)
a0-your-suffix-pronouns.pattern: noun + ־ךָ (talking to a man) / noun + ־ךְ (talking to a woman) = "your ___"
a0-your-suffix-pronouns.explanation: Hebrew's "how are you?" is literally "what is your welfare?" — but "your" isn't a separate word here, it's a suffix glued straight onto the noun שָׁלוֹם (welfare/peace). Speaking to a man you add ־ךָ, giving שְׁלוֹמְךָ (shlomcha); speaking to a woman you add ־ךְ, giving שְׁלוֹמֵךְ (shlomech). This is your first taste of Hebrew's possessive suffixes, which later attach to many other nouns the same way.
a0-your-suffix-pronouns.fact: Instead of a separate possessive word like English "your," Hebrew fuses the pronoun directly onto the end of the noun — a pattern going back over 3,000 years to Biblical Hebrew, and shared with Arabic's similar suffixes -ak/-ik.

a0-number-gender-agreement.title: Numbers Match Gender (הַתְאָמַת מִסְפָּרִים בְּמִגְדָּר)
a0-number-gender-agreement.explanation: Hebrew numbers 1–10 have two forms: one for masculine nouns and one for feminine nouns. 'Three' is שְׁלוֹשָׁה (shloshah) before a masculine noun like יְלָדִים (boys), but שָׁלוֹשׁ (shalosh) before a feminine noun like בָּנוֹת (girls). Curiously, the masculine form of the number often carries a feminine-looking ה ending, and the feminine form often looks 'bare' — the opposite of what you'd expect. Numbers 11-19 follow the same pattern in their units digit, while 20 (עֶשְׂרִים) stays the same for both genders.
a0-number-gender-agreement.fact: This swap is called 'gender polarity,' and Hebrew is one of the few languages where cardinal numbers systematically invert gender markers with the nouns they count — a masculine noun takes a number that looks feminine, and a feminine noun takes a number that looks masculine.

a0-on-day-of-week.title: Saying 'On Sunday' (בְּ + יוֹם)
a0-on-day-of-week.explanation: To say something happens on a certain day, attach the prefix בְּ ('be-', meaning on/in/at) directly onto יוֹם plus the day's name, e.g. בְּיוֹם רִאשׁוֹן ('on Sunday'). Shabbat is the exception — it doesn't take יוֹם, so you simply say בְּשַׁבָּת ('on Shabbat'). The very same בְּ prefix is also used for parts of the day: בַּבֹּקֶר ('in the morning'), בָּעֶרֶב ('in the evening').
a0-on-day-of-week.fact: Hebrew doesn't have unique names for most weekdays — Sunday through Thursday are literally 'Day One' (יוֹם רִאשׁוֹן) through 'Day Five' (יוֹם חֲמִישִׁי), counted from the day after Shabbat, the only day of the week with a true name of its own.

a0-possessive-sheli.title: Possessive Pronouns with שֶׁל ("Mine, Yours, His, Hers")
a0-possessive-sheli.pattern: Noun + שֶׁלִּי / שֶׁלְּךָ / שֶׁלָּךְ / שֶׁלּוֹ / שֶׁלָּה
a0-possessive-sheli.explanation: Hebrew shows possession by placing the noun first and adding a form of שֶׁל ("of, belonging to") right after it, with a pronoun ending attached: שֶׁלִּי (my), שֶׁלְּךָ (your, to a man), שֶׁלָּךְ (your, to a woman), שֶׁלּוֹ (his), שֶׁלָּה (her). Unlike English "my dad," Hebrew literally says "dad of-mine": אַבָּא שֶׁלִּי. Close family words like אבא/אמא usually stay without "the," while other nouns often keep the definite article before them: הַדּוֹד שֶׁלּוֹ ("his uncle").
a0-possessive-sheli.fact: שֶׁל is a fairly late fusion in Hebrew's history — it grew out of the older phrase אֲשֶׁר לְ ("that which belongs to"), which speakers gradually compressed over the centuries into the single tiny word שֶׁל that's used constantly in Modern Hebrew today.

a0-adjective-agreement.title: Adjectives Follow the Noun & Match Its Gender (הַתְאָמַת שֵׁם תֹּאַר)
a0-adjective-agreement.pattern: noun + adjective — the adjective comes right after the noun and changes its ending to match the noun's gender and number
a0-adjective-agreement.explanation: In Hebrew, a describing word (adjective) is placed after the noun it describes, so 'a red apple' is literally 'apple red' — תַּפּוּחַ אָדֹם. Adjectives also change form to agree with the noun: masculine singular nouns take the plain form (אָדֹם), feminine singular nouns usually add ה- at the end (אֲדֻמָּה), and plural nouns add ים- or ות- (אֲדֻמִּים / אֲדֻמּוֹת). This rule applies to color words and to almost all other Hebrew adjectives, like 'big' and 'new'.
a0-adjective-agreement.fact: Some Hebrew color words are ancient — אָדֹם (red) and לָבָן (white) appear already in the Bible — while others like כָּתֹם (orange) and סָגֹל (purple) are much newer, coined or standardized only during the modern revival of Hebrew in the 19th–20th centuries.

a0-hurts-li.title: Expressing pain: כּוֹאֵב לִי (it hurts me)
a0-hurts-li.pattern: [body part] + כּוֹאֵב / כּוֹאֶבֶת + לִי / לְךָ / לָךְ / לוֹ / לָהּ
a0-hurts-li.explanation: Hebrew doesn't say 'my head hurts' the way English does. Instead, the body part is the grammatical subject, and the person feeling the pain is marked with the word לְ ('to') plus a pronoun ending, attached after the verb. So 'my head hurts' is literally 'the head hurts to-me' (הָרֹאשׁ כּוֹאֵב לִי). The verb כּוֹאֵב/כּוֹאֶבֶת always agrees in gender with the body part, not with the person who feels the pain.
a0-hurts-li.fact: This 'to me it hurts' pattern is called a dative-experiencer construction, and Hebrew uses the exact same לִי/לְךָ/לוֹ/לָהּ pattern for liking things, being cold, and needing something — so learning it here for body parts unlocks a whole family of everyday Hebrew sentences.

a0-verb-gender-agreement.title: Present-tense verbs agree with gender: אוֹהֵב / אוֹהֶבֶת (to like)
a0-verb-gender-agreement.pattern: m. singular: אוֹהֵב | f. singular: אוֹהֶבֶת | plural (any gender mix): אוֹהֲבִים
a0-verb-gender-agreement.explanation: In Hebrew, present-tense verbs change their ending to match the gender and number of the subject, not the tense. The verb for 'like/love' becomes אוֹהֵב after a masculine subject (אֲנִי, אַתָּה, הוּא) but אוֹהֶבֶת after a feminine subject (אֲנִי, אַתְּ, הִיא) — the same word 'אֲנִי' can trigger either form depending on the speaker's gender. For plural subjects, the form is אוֹהֲבִים regardless of gender mix. This same gender-agreement pattern applies to almost every Hebrew verb in the present tense.
a0-verb-gender-agreement.fact: Hebrew present-tense verbs are historically participles (בֵּינוֹנִי), which is exactly why they agree with gender and number like adjectives do — English 'I like / she likes' changes for person, but Hebrew verbs don't care about person at all, only about gender and number.

a0-demonstratives-here-there.title: This/That & Here/There (זֶה / זֹאת / כָּאן / שָׁם)
a0-demonstratives-here-there.pattern: זֶה / זֹאת + noun = "this is __"; noun + הַזֶּה / הַזֹּאת / הַהוּא / הַהִיא = "this/that __"; כָּאן / שָׁם = here / there
a0-demonstratives-here-there.explanation: Hebrew has two words for 'this': זֶה for masculine nouns and זֹאת for feminine nouns — the word must match the gender of the noun it points to, not the gender of the speaker. When 'this' or 'that' describes a noun rather than just pointing at it, it moves after the noun and gets its own -הַ, as in הַסֵּפֶר הַזֶּה ('this book') or הַכּוֹס הַהִיא ('that cup'). The location words כָּאן (here) and שָׁם (there) never change form, no matter the gender or number of what they describe.
a0-demonstratives-here-there.fact: In everyday spoken Hebrew, זֶה is one of the most overused words in the language — beyond meaning 'this', Israelis sprinkle it into sentences the way English speakers use 'so' or 'like', as in זֶה... אֲנִי לֹא יוֹדֵעַ ('so... I don't know').

a0-questions.title: Yes/No and Wh-Questions (הַאִם, מָה, מִי, אֵיפֹה)
a0-questions.pattern: הַאִם + statement = yes/no question; מָה / מִי / אֵיפֹה + statement = wh-question
a0-questions.explanation: To turn a statement into a yes/no question in Hebrew, add הַאִם ('is it that...') to the front, or simply raise your voice at the end and add a question mark — Hebrew doesn't reorder the words the way English does. To ask for specific information, start with a question word — מָה ('what'), מִי ('who'), or אֵיפֹה ('where') — followed by the rest of the sentence in normal statement word order.
a0-questions.fact: In everyday spoken Hebrew, הַאִם is very often dropped entirely — "אַתָּה עָיֵף?" ("You're tired?") said with rising intonation is just as correct and common as "הַאִם אַתָּה עָיֵף?", especially in casual conversation.

a0-pronoun-to-be.title: Personal Pronouns as 'To Be' (מִשְׁפָּט שֵׁמִי / Nominal Sentence)
a0-pronoun-to-be.explanation: Hebrew has no present-tense verb for 'to be'. To say 'I am tired' or 'She is a teacher', you just put the subject pronoun directly next to the adjective or noun: אֲנִי עָיֵף literally reads 'I tired' but means 'I am tired'. The verb לִהְיוֹת (to be) only shows up in the past and future tenses — the present tense sentence needs no verb at all.
a0-pronoun-to-be.fact: This verbless 'is/am/are' structure is called a מִשְׁפָּט שֵׁמִי (nominal sentence) and has existed since Biblical Hebrew — you'll see the exact same pattern in Genesis 3 millennia ago.

a0-present-tense-verbs.title: Present Tense Verbs (הֹוֶה)
a0-present-tense-verbs.pattern: subject + verb (verb agrees with gender & number only, not person)
a0-present-tense-verbs.explanation: Hebrew present-tense verbs don't change for 'I / you / he' the way English verbs sometimes do — they only change for gender (masculine/feminine) and number (singular/plural), just like adjectives do. That means אֲנִי, אַתָּה, and הוּא can all share the exact same verb form (e.g. הוֹלֵךְ), while אֲנִי and הִיא use different forms (הוֹלֵךְ vs. הוֹלֶכֶת) purely because of gender. There is also no separate word for 'am/is/are' — the verb itself already carries that meaning.
a0-present-tense-verbs.fact: Hebrew's present tense actually developed from an ancient participle (similar to English '-ing' forms), which is why it behaves grammatically like an adjective — agreeing with gender and number — instead of conjugating for person the way the past and future tenses do.

a0-yesh-there-is.title: There is / There are (יֵשׁ)
a0-yesh-there-is.explanation: Hebrew uses one small word, יֵשׁ, to say 'there is' or 'there are' — it never changes for gender or number like verbs do. To say something doesn't exist, swap in אֵין instead. Add a location with the prefix בְּ ('in') to say where something is, as in יֵשׁ שֻׁלְחָן בַּמִּטְבָּח ('there is a table in the kitchen').
a0-yesh-there-is.fact: יֵשׁ and אֵין aren't verbs at all — they're invisible in the past and future tense, where Hebrew switches to forms of הָיָה ('was'), so beginners only meet this handy shortcut in the present.

a0-dative-feelings-weather.title: Feeling Hot or Cold: The "To Me" Pattern (קַר לִי / חַם לוֹ)
a0-dative-feelings-weather.pattern: [weather adjective] + לְ + pronoun suffix  (e.g. קַר לִי, חַם לָהּ, נָעִים לָנוּ)
a0-dative-feelings-weather.explanation: To say you feel hot, cold, or comfortable, Hebrew does not use a normal subject + "to be" sentence like English "I am cold." Instead, it puts the adjective first and attaches the feeling to a person using לְ ("to") plus a pronoun ending: קַר לִי literally means "cold to-me." The person who feels it is marked by the preposition, not by a subject pronoun — so לִי, לְךָ, לָהּ, לוֹ, לָנוּ each swap in depending on who feels the weather.
a0-dative-feelings-weather.fact: This same "to-me" pattern shows up all over Hebrew feelings and needs — חָסֵר לִי ("I'm missing something"), כּוֹאֵב לִי ("it hurts me") — and it closely parallels constructions in Spanish (tengo frío-style logic), Russian, and Yiddish, where the person experiencing a sensation is grammatically a receiver rather than a doer.

a0-directional-heh.title: The Directional Suffix -ה (ה' הַכִּוּוּן)
a0-directional-heh.pattern: direction word + ה (unstressed) = movement toward it: יָמִין → יָמִינָה, שְׂמֹאל → שְׂמֹאלָה
a0-directional-heh.explanation: Adding an unstressed ה at the end of certain Hebrew words turns them into 'toward' words — it answers the question 'to where?' instead of 'where?'. So יָמִין ("right") becomes יָמִינָה ("to the right"), and שְׂמֹאל ("left") becomes שְׂמֹאלָה ("to the left"). This final ה is not the feminine ending — it's silent-ish and unstressed, and it only ever attaches to nouns/directions to show motion toward them.
a0-directional-heh.fact: This is one of the oldest surviving features of Hebrew grammar: the very same directional heh shows up in the Bible, where מִצְרַיִם ("Egypt") becomes מִצְרַיְמָה, meaning "to Egypt" — the exact same trick used today in ימינה and שמאלה.

a1-piel-hifil-present.title: Present tense in Pi'el and Hif'il verbs (בִּנְיָן פִּעֵל וְהִפְעִיל בַּהוֹוֶה)
a1-piel-hifil-present.pattern: Pa'al present tense has no prefix (קוֹרֵא, כּוֹתֵב); Pi'el and Hif'il present tense both add the prefix מְ- (מְדַבֵּר, מֵבִין) — and every present-tense verb, in any binyan, still changes for gender and number.
a1-piel-hifil-present.explanation: Hebrew verbs belong to different patterns called binyanim. In the present tense, Pa'al verbs like לִקְרוֹא (read) and לִכְתּוֹב (write) use no prefix — the form is just the root with vowels. But Pi'el verbs like לְדַבֵּר (speak) and Hif'il verbs like לְהָבִין (understand) both add a מְ- prefix instead: מְדַבֵּר, מֵבִין. Despite this difference, every present-tense verb still has four forms for masculine/feminine, singular/plural, e.g. מְדַבֵּר / מְדַבֶּרֶת / מְדַבְּרִים / מְדַבְּרוֹת.
a1-piel-hifil-present.fact: The מְ- prefix on present-tense Pi'el and Hif'il verbs is actually a fossilized participle marker — the same מ- that appears on Hebrew nouns meaning 'one who does X', like מוֹרֶה (teacher) and מְנַהֵל (manager).

lessons.title: Good Morning at School
lessons.description: Dani arrives late to class and greets his teacher.

readingPassage.context: Dani arrives late to class and greets his teacher.

paragraphs.native: דָּנִי נִכְנָס לַכִּתָּה וְאוֹמֵר: "בֹּקֶר טוֹב לְכֻלָּם!"
paragraphs.en: Dani walks into the classroom and says, "Good morning, everyone!"

paragraphs.native: הַמּוֹרָה אוֹמֶרֶת: "בֹּקֶר טוֹב, דָּנִי, מָה שְׁלוֹמְךָ?"
paragraphs.en: The teacher says, "Good morning, Dani, how are you?"

paragraphs.native: דָּנִי אוֹמֵר: "טוֹב מְאֹד, תּוֹדָה! סְלִיחָה עַל הָאִחוּר."
paragraphs.en: Dani says, "Very well, thank you! Sorry I'm late."

paragraphs.en: Dana loves winter.

paragraphs.native: הִיא אוֹמֶרֶת: "קַר לִי, אֲבָל זֶה נָעִים!"
paragraphs.en: She says: "I'm cold, but it's nice!"

paragraphs.native: בַּקַּיִץ חַם מְאֹד, וְדָנָה מַעֲדִיפָה אֶת הַגֶּשֶׁם וְאֶת הַשֶּׁלֶג.
paragraphs.en: In summer it's very hot, and Dana prefers the rain and the snow.

lessons.title: Looking for the Market
lessons.description: Dana is walking in the city and asks a stranger for directions to the market.

readingPassage.context: Dana is walking in the city and asks a stranger for directions to the market.

paragraphs.native: דָּנָה מְחַפֶּשֶׂת אֶת הַשּׁוּק בָּעִיר.
paragraphs.en: Dana is looking for the market in the city.

paragraphs.native: אִישׁ אוֹמֵר לָהּ: "לְכִי יָשָׁר וּפְנִי יָמִינָה."
paragraphs.en: A man tells her: "Go straight and turn right."

paragraphs.native: הַשּׁוּק לֹא רָחוֹק, הוּא קָרוֹב לַפִּנָּה.
paragraphs.en: The market is not far, it's near the corner.

lessons.title: An Ordinary Day
lessons.description: A short story about Dana's daily routine, tying together greetings, family, and everyday phrases from this level.

readingPassage.context: A short story about Dana's daily routine, tying together greetings, family, and everyday phrases from this level.

paragraphs.native: בַּבֹּקֶר דָּנָה קָמָה וְאוֹכֶלֶת אֲרוּחַת בֹּקֶר עִם הַמִּשְׁפָּחָה שֶׁלָּהּ.
paragraphs.en: In the morning, Dana gets up and eats breakfast with her family.

paragraphs.native: אַחַר כָּךְ הִיא הוֹלֶכֶת לְבֵית הַסֵּפֶר וּפוֹגֶשֶׁת אֶת הַחֲבֵרָה הַטּוֹבָה שֶׁלָּהּ.
paragraphs.en: Afterwards she goes to school and meets her good friend.

paragraphs.native: בָּעֶרֶב הֵן חוֹזְרוֹת הַבַּיְתָה וְאוֹמְרוֹת: "לְהִתְרָאוֹת מָחָר!"
paragraphs.en: In the evening they return home and say: "See you tomorrow!"

lessons.title: A New Job
lessons.description: A short story about Noa, who is learning Hebrew at her new job in Tel Aviv.

readingPassage.context: A short story about Noa, who is learning Hebrew at her new job in Tel Aviv.

paragraphs.native: נוֹעָה עוֹבֶדֶת בְּמִשְׂרָד גָּדוֹל בְּתֵל אָבִיב.
paragraphs.en: Noa works in a big office in Tel Aviv.

paragraphs.native: בָּעֲבוֹדָה הִיא מְדַבֶּרֶת עִבְרִית עִם הַחֲבֵרִים שֶׁלָּה, אֲבָל הִיא עֲדַיִן לֹא מְבִינָה הַכֹּל.
paragraphs.en: At work she speaks Hebrew with her friends, but she still doesn't understand everything.

paragraphs.native: כָּל בֹּקֶר הִיא קוֹרֵאת עִתּוֹן בְּעִבְרִית וְכוֹתֶבֶת מִלִּים חֲדָשׁוֹת בְּמַחְבֶּרֶת.
paragraphs.en: Every morning she reads a newspaper in Hebrew and writes new words in a notebook.

paragraphs.native: הַחֲבֵרִים שֶׁלָּה בָּעֲבוֹדָה עוֹזְרִים לָהּ, וְהִיא מְבִינָה יוֹתֵר וְיוֹתֵר כָּל שָׁבוּעַ.
paragraphs.en: Her friends at work help her, and she understands more and more every week.

exercises.question: Where does Noa work?
options: In a big office in Tel Aviv

exercises.question: What does Noa do every morning?
options: She writes a letter to her mom
She reads a newspaper and writes new words in a notebook
She speaks English with her boss
She reads a book about Tel Aviv

exercises.question: According to the passage, how does Noa's Hebrew understanding change over time?
options: It gets better and better every week
